sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=23, col=29).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 46, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 28 of about 42 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, now I see what happened:
- Prev W pixels: 28, Curr W pixels: 26 (white strip decreased by 2)
- Prev B pixels: 3343, Curr B pixels: 3345 (black background increased by 2)
- Blob pixels: 69 → 69 (unchanged)
- M squares: all unchanged
So clicking (23, 29) did NOT move any square! It only changed the white strip (HUD) and the black background. This is a wasted action - the click didn't affect any gameplay objects.
This means (23, 29) is NOT a valid click position for moving squares. But why? The gameplay area is rows 10-62, and (23, 29) is within this range.
Wait, maybe the issue is that (23, 29) is on the BLUE BLOB, and clicking on the blob doesn't move squares. Let me check: the blob boundary is [[23, 31], [23, 35], [25, 37], [29, 37], [31, 35], [31, 31], [29, 29], [25, 29]]. The blob starts at col 31 at row 23. So (23, 29) is NOT on the blob (it's to the left of the blob).
Hmm, but the blob's boundary includes [25, 29] and [29, 29]. So at rows 25-29, the blob extends to col 29. But at row 23, the blob starts at col 31. So (23, 29) is in the black background area, not on the blob.
So why didn't clicking (23, 29) move any square? Let me reconsider the action model.
Wait, maybe I had the action model wrong. Let me re-examine the successful moves:
1. Click (31, 37): moved sq2 from (36-37, 40-41) to (30-31, 36-37). The clicked cell (31, 37) became the bottom-right corner of sq2.
2. Click (26, 9): moved sq1 from (26-27, 9-10) to (25-26, 8-9). The clicked cell (26, 9) became the bottom-right corner of sq1.
3. Click (26, 10): moved sq1 from (25-26, 8-9) to (26-27, 9-10). The clicked cell (26, 10) became the bottom-right corner of sq1.
So the action model is: clicking a cell moves the nearest M square so the clicked cell becomes its bottom-right corner.
But clicking (23, 29) didn't move any square. Why?
Maybe the issue is that (23, 29) is too far from all squares, and the game has a maximum movement distance? Let me check:
- Dist to sq1 (26.5, 9.5): |23-26.5| + |29-9.5| = 3.5 + 19.5 = 23
- Dist to sq2 (36.5, 40.5): |23-36.5| + |29-40.5| = 13.5 + 11.5 = 25
- Dist to sq3 (53.5, 48.5): |23-53.5| + |29-48.5| = 30.5 + 19.5 = 50
- Dist to sq4 (54.5, 13.5): |23-54.5| + |29-13.5| = 31.5 + 15.5 = 47
The closest square is sq1 at distance 23. But the click didn't move sq1.
Hmm, maybe there's a maximum distance for square movement. Let me check the successful moves:
- Click (31, 37) moved sq2 from (36.5, 40.5) to (30.5, 36.5). Distance: |31-36.5| + |37-40.5| = 5.5 + 3.5 = 9.
- Click (26, 9) moved sq1 from (26.5, 9.5) to (25.5, 8.5). Distance: |26-26.5| + |9-9.5| = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.
- Click (26, 10) moved sq1 from (25.5, 8.5) to (26.5, 9.5). Distance: |26-25.5| + |10-8.5| = 0.5 + 1.5 = 2.
So the successful moves had distances 1, 2, and 9. The failed move (23, 29) had a distance of 23 to the nearest square. Maybe there's a maximum distance of, say, 10 or 15?
Let me test this hypothesis by clicking closer to a square.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the click (31, 37) that moved sq2. Before that click, sq2 was at (36-37, 40-41). The click moved it to (30-31, 36-37). The distance from sq2's center (36.5, 40.5) to the click (31, 37) is |31-36.5| + |37-40.5| = 5.5 + 3.5 = 9.
But wait, the click (31, 37) is within the blob area (blob is rows 23-31, cols 29-37). So maybe clicking on the blob DOES move squares, but only if the square is close enough.
Let me try clicking closer to sq1. Sq1 is at (26-27, 9-10). Let me click at (27, 15), which is 5 columns to the right of sq1.
- Dist to sq1 (26.5, 9.5): |27-26.5| + |15-9.5| = 0.5 + 5.5 = 6
- Dist to sq2 (36.5, 40.5): |27-36.5| + |15-40.5| = 9.5 + 25.5 = 35
- Dist to sq4 (54.5, 13.5): |27-54.5| + |15-13.5| = 27.5 + 1.5 = 29
Sq1 is closest. If the max distance is > 6, this should work.
Let me try this.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Test: click closer to sq1 to see if distance matters
# Sq1 is at (26-27, 9-10), center (26.5, 9.5)
# Click at (27, 15), distance ~6

result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 27, 'col': 15}])
print(f"Click (27,15): board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("\nM squares after:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4 and n['boundary'][0][0] >= 10:
        print(f"  {n['boundary']}")

# Check W strip
w_nodes = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'W']
print(f"\nW pixels: {[n['pixels'] for n in w_nodes]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
